perturbation.original_text: Each party shall comply with all applicable US and other export laws, regulations and rules and, in particular, will not export or re -export Confidential Information without obtaining all required government licenses, approvals or waivers .
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall comply with some export guidelines and regulations as they deem appropriate, and may export or re-export Confidential Information without explicit government approval based on their own risk assessment.
perturbation.explanation: This modification suggests that parties have the discretion to decide which export regulations to follow and can export without government approval. This contradicts the strict requirements of export control laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR requires strict adherence to licensing requirements for exporting controlled items and technologies. The modified text allows for discretionary compliance and unlicensed export, directly contradicting this regulation.

perturbation.justification: The changed text allows parties to comply with only 'some export guidelines and regulations as they deem appropriate' and to export without 'explicit government approval based on their own risk assessment.' This contradicts the general principle, as exemplified by the snippet from 15 CFR 732.1, that export regulations are mandatory and not subject to discretionary compliance. The change undermines the legal obligation to adhere to all applicable export laws, creating a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All disputes, controversies or claims between the parties arising out of or in connection with this Agreement (including its existence, validity or termination) shall be finally resolved by arbitration to be held in Seoul, Korea and conducted in English under the Rules of Arbitration of the Intern ational Chamber of Commerce ; provided, however, that each party may enforce its or its affiliates’ intellectual property rights in any court of competent jurisdiction, including but not limit ed to equitable relief .
perturbation.changed_text: All disputes, controversies or claims between the parties arising out of or in connection with this Agreement (including its existence, validity or termination) shall be resolved through mandatory mediation. If mediation fails, disputes can then proceed to litigation in a court of the Discloser's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies mandatory arbitration in Seoul, Korea. The modified clause replaces this with mandatory mediation followed by litigation, potentially in a jurisdiction different from Seoul, Korea, fundamentally altering the dispute resolution process. Also by giving the option to the 'Discloser' the term creates an imbalance and can be unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FAA favors the enforcement of arbitration agreements. By replacing arbitration with mediation and litigation, the modified text undermines the original agreement to arbitrate, potentially conflicting with the FAA's policy of upholding arbitration agreements.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates arbitration in Seoul, while the modified text mandates mediation and potential litigation in a court chosen by the 'Discloser'. This directly contradicts the FAA's policy favoring the enforcement of arbitration agreements. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, both citing 9 U.S.C. § 2, state that agreements to arbitrate are 'valid, irrevocable, and enforceable'. By replacing arbitration with mediation and litigation, the modified text undermines the original agreement to arbitrate, conflicting with the FAA's policy of upholding arbitration agreements, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall, upon termination of this Agreement, or upon written request of Discloser, whichever is earlier, immediately, but not later than 10 days after any notice thereof by Discloser, return (or destroy at Discloser’s option) all copies of such Discloser’s Confidential Information and certify in writing its compliance with this requirement, except that Recipient may retain a copy of such Confidential Information solely for archival purpose .
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall, upon termination of this Agreement, or upon written request of Discloser, whichever is earlier, return all copies of such Discloser’s Confidential Information; however, the Recipient is not required to destroy, return, or certify the destruction or returning of such information and may retain copies indefinitely for business continuity purposes.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires the recipient to return or destroy confidential information upon termination and to certify compliance. The modified clause removes the requirement for destruction/return/certification and allows indefinite retention for business continuity, which contradicts the intent of confidentiality and data protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Trade Secrets Act primarily addresses misappropriation, allowing indefinite retention of confidential information significantly increases the risk of misappropriation and weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets, contradicting the spirit and purpose of the law.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the intent of protecting trade secrets. The original text ensures the return or destruction of confidential information upon termination, whereas the changed text allows indefinite retention, significantly increasing the risk of misappropriation. While the scraped snippet 18 USC 1836 primarily addresses civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation, the allowance of indefinite retention fundamentally undermines the protection afforded to trade secrets, as it removes a key safeguard against potential misuse.

perturbation.original_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, “affiliate ” means an entity that , as of Effective Date , directly or indirectly, controls or is under common control with a party to this Agreement, but only for so long as such control exists , and where “control” shall mean ownership of more than 50% of the stock or other equity interests entitled to vote for the election of directors or an equivalent governing body.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, “affiliate” means any entity that the party deems to be affiliated, at their sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'affiliate' is based on objective criteria (ownership of more than 50% of voting stock). The modified definition makes affiliation entirely subjective and at the party's sole discretion. This is problematic, because it can mean one party considers certain companies as affiliates and the other does not, and that can affect liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Many aspects of corporate law, including control share acquisition statutes like DGCL § 203, rely on objective definitions of 'control'. The subjective definition of 'affiliate' and thus potentially 'control' creates uncertainty and undermines established corporate law principles.

Neither paragraph (g)(7)(A) and (B) of this section nor any provision of a surviving entityâs organizational documents required by paragraph (g)(7)(A) and (B) of this section shall be deemed or construed to require approval of the stockholders of the holding company to elect or remove directors or managers, managing members or other members of the governing body of the surviving entity. The term âorganizational documentsâ, as used in paragraph (g)(7) of this section and in the preceding sentence, shall, when used in reference to a corporation, mean the certificate of incorporation of such corporation, and when used in reference to a limited liability company, mean the limited liability company agreement of such limited liability company. From and after the effective time of a merger adopted by a constituent corporation by action of its board of directors and without any vote of stockholders pursuant to this subsection: (i) to the extent the restrictions of Â§â203 of this title applied to the constituent corporation and its stockholders at the effective time of the merger, such restrictions shall apply to the holding company and its stockholders immediately after the effective time of the merger as though it were the constituent corporation, and all shares of stock of the holding company acquired in the merger shall for purposes of Â§â203 of this title be deemed to have been acquired at the time that the shares of stock of the constituent corporation converted in the merger were acquired, and provided further that any stockholder who immediately prior to the effective time of the merger was not an interested stockholder within the meaning of Â§â203 of this title shall not solely by reason of the merger become an interested stockholder of the holding company, (ii) if the corporate name of the holding company immediately following the effective time of the merger is the same as the corporate name of the constituent corporation immediately prior to the effective time of the merger, the shares of capital stock of the holding company into which the shares of capital stock of the constituent corporation are converted in the merger shall be represented by the stock certificates that previously represented shares of capital stock of the constituent corporation and (iii) to the extent a stockholder of the constituent corporation immediately prior to the merger had standing to institute or maintain derivative litigation on behalf of the constituent corporation, nothing in this section shall be deemed to limit or extinguish such standing. If an agreement of merger is adopted by a constituent corporation by action of its board of directors and without any vote of stockholders pursuant to this subsection, the secretary or assistant secretary of the constituent corporation shall certify on the agreement that the agreement has been adopted pursuant to this subsection and that the conditions specified in the first sentence of this subse
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text, which defines 'affiliate' at a party's sole discretion, creates a legal contradiction. The law explanation states that objective definitions of 'control' are relied upon in corporate law, like DGCL § 203. The scraped law snippet 1 refers to Â§â203 and its application to holding companies, implying the importance of a clear definition of control and affiliation for legal compliance. The original contract's objective criteria for 'affiliate' aligns with this legal principle, while the modified subjective definition undermines it, potentially leading to uncertainty and non-compliance with regulations that rely on objective definitions of affiliation.

perturbation.original_text: Each party acknowledges that any disclosure, use or misappropriation of Confidential Inform ation of another party in violation of this Agreement would cause such party irreparable harm for which there may be no adequate remedy at law. Accordingly, each party agrees that such other party shall have the right to apply to any court of competent jur isdiction for injunctive relief and specific performance, without prejudice to any remedies available to it at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party acknowledges that any disclosure, use or misappropriation of Confidential Information is unlikely to cause irreparable harm, and that monetary damages will be sufficient in all cases. Injunctive relief is explicitly waived by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause acknowledges the potential for irreparable harm and preserves the right to seek injunctive relief. The modified clause denies the possibility of irreparable harm and waives the right to injunctive relief. This significantly weakens the remedies available for breach of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA allows for injunctive relief in cases of actual or threatened misappropriation of trade secrets. By waiving injunctive relief, the modified text limits the remedies available under UTSA, potentially rendering the agreement less effective in protecting trade secrets and creating confusion.

perturbation.justification: The modification explicitly waives the right to injunctive relief, which UTSA (as referenced in the Law Explanation) allows for in cases of actual or threatened misappropriation of trade secrets. By stating that 'Injunctive relief is explicitly waived by both parties,' the changed text directly contradicts the potential availability of injunctive relief under UTSA, creating a legal contradiction. The original contract preserved the right to apply to any court of competent jurisdiction for injunctive relief and specific performance while the new one doesn't, increasing risk of trade secret misappropriation.
